bio.title: Doctor
bio.photo_notes: Doctor Robert Morris (1809-1904), surgeon of the 91st NY Infantry

education.notes: One of the preeminent medical schools in New York.

career.notes: Retired at age 94. Most of professional life in Ogdensburg, but practiced partnership periods.

military_service.notes: Severely injured at Key West FL when thrown from horse while stationed there.

narrative_blocks.header: Translating Latin from memory at age ninety-five
narrative_blocks.body: Two months before his death in November 1904, confined to his bed in Ogdensburg, Dr. Robert Morris sent for a volume from his library and read from it aloud, translating the classical Latin text 'with a readiness and correctness that were most remarkable in one so old,' according to the 1905 tribute published in the Albany Medical Annals. His mind held perfectly at the end of a life that had crossed from Scotland to America, from a Hamilton County farm to a medical college, and from a Civil War field hospital to six decades of practice in the same St. Lawrence River city.

narrative_blocks.header: Thrown from his horse while serving as surgeon
narrative_blocks.body: As surgeon of the 91st New York Infantry, Dr. Robert Morris was 'quite severely injured while stationed at Key West, Florida, by being thrown from his horse' — a wound that did not curtail his career but rather introduced a note of battlefield contingency into an otherwise steady civilian practice. He had trained at Albany Medical College, graduating in 1846, and the Civil War interruption was the one major departure from the Ogdensburg practice he would otherwise sustain for nearly sixty years.

narrative_blocks.header: Retired at ninety-four; his practice never left Ogdensburg
narrative_blocks.body: Dr. Robert Morris retired from his Ogdensburg practice in 1903 at the age of ninety-four — one of the longest documented medical careers in northern New York — and died the following year, having spent the better part of six decades within a single city on the St. Lawrence River. His wife Lucy Case Hooker Morris died in 1856 at thirty-five; he outlived her for forty-eight years, longer than she had lived.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Scotland; a surgeon six decades in Ogdensburg
narrative_blocks.body: Robert Morris was born in Scotland in 1809 and came to America as a child, his parents settling as farmers in Hammond, New York — a passage from the Scottish countryside to the St. Lawrence frontier that would have been unremarkable to the thousands of Scots who made that journey in the Napoleonic era, except that this particular boy would end up training at Albany Medical College, marrying into the founding family of Irondequoit's Hooker Cemetery, and practicing medicine until he was ninety-four. He entered the Hooker orbit entirely by geography and profession, but left a record that belongs, through his marriage to Lucy Case Hooker Morris, to the Hooker family's documentation of itself.

sources.label: Albany Medical Annals — Tribute to Dr. Robert Morris (1905), Dr. J. Montgomery Mosher, ed.

naming_inspiration.description: Named for her mother Lucy Case Hooker.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)

narrative_blocks.header: Mother died when she was three
narrative_blocks.body: Mary C. Morris lost her mother Lucy Case Hooker Morris in October 1856 when she was barely three years old, and grew up in Ogdensburg under the care of her father, Doctor Robert Morris — the Civil War surgeon who would practice medicine until age ninety-four. She outlived her father by thirty-three years and her mother by eighty-one, dying in 1937 at age eighty-three, her life a quiet arc from the Irondequoit frontier her grandfather Alexander Allin Hooker had helped settle to a twentieth-century death far from the Hooker Cemetery he had deeded to the town.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909)
